title: Cascaded Rydberg antiblockade: Multi-atom excitation dynamics and entanglement
authors: Jin-Lei Wu, Jun Wu, Pei-Yao Song, Yan Wang, Ya Gao, Xue-Ke Song, Shi-Lei Su
abstract: We propose a cascaded Rydberg antiblockade (RAB) regime via a Floquet modulation in four fully connected interacting atoms, which establishes a new synthetic dimension, Dicke-state lattice (DSL), in the space of collective spin excitations. By applying a global periodic driving, we synthesize an effective Hamiltonian that enables perfect state transfer across the five-site DSL with multiple programmable pathways from stepwise nearest-neighbor jumps to a single-step transition. This DSL platform further allows us to simulate a dynamic Su-Schrieffer-Heeger model, where soft quantum control is employed to achieve topologically inspired full RAB $|0000\rangle \to |1111\rangle$ with enhanced robustness against disorder. Moreover, by incorporating the shortcut to adiabaticity technique, we generate high-fidelity entangled twin-Fock and Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger states on the four atoms within sub-microsecond timescales, outperforming the speed limits of conventional adiabatic protocols. Our work demonstrates a flexible and programmable synthetic dimension for quantum simulation and multipartite entanglement engineering in Rydberg atom arrays, paving the way for the future development of quantum information processing.
summary: The authors propose using Floquet modulation on four interacting Rydberg atoms to create a controllable Dicke-state lattice. They demonstrate advanced quantum control techniques, including soft quantum control and STA, to achieve perfect state transfer and generate high-fidelity twin-Fock and GHZ entangled states rapidly. This establishes the platform as a versatile tool for quantum simulation and entanglement engineering.
main_problem: The research aims to achieve coherent, programmable control over collective spin excitation dynamics in interacting Rydberg atom arrays using a cascaded Rydberg antiblockade (RAB) regime.
main_result: They successfully demonstrate perfect state transfer across a synthetic Dicke-state lattice and generate high-fidelity entangled states (twin-Fock and GHZ) on sub-microsecond timescales, outperforming conventional adiabatic methods.
method: The study employs Floquet modulation to derive an effective Hamiltonian for the system, utilizing techniques like soft quantum control and Stimulated Raman Adiabatic Passage (STA).
model_or_system: The platform uses four fully connected interacting Rydberg atoms in $^{87}	ext{Rb}$ trapped in a lattice. The dynamics are modeled using a master equation incorporating spontaneous decay, leading to an effective Hamiltonian describing the Dicke-state lattice.
key_observables: Population transfer fidelity ($|0000angle 	o |1111angle$), Concurrence ($	ext{CN}$) for twin-Fock and GHZ states, and population dynamics across the DSL sites.
important_parameters: Rydberg blockade strength ($V$), driving field modulation parameters ($\Omega_n(t)$), and timescales (sub-microsecond).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on applying the Rotating-Wave Approximation (RWA) and specific conditions for adiabatic elimination to derive the effective Hamiltonian.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate population transfers across the DSL, compare soft control vs. conventional methods for state transfer fidelity, and show the evolution of entanglement concurrence for generated states.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from defining the initial system Hamiltonian under global periodic driving to deriving an effective Dicke-state lattice Hamiltonian. It then details applications: first, demonstrating perfect state transfer using soft control; second, simulating topological dynamics; and finally, generating high-fidelity entanglement via STA.
why_interesting: This work provides a highly programmable synthetic dimension (DSL) for quantum simulation, offering robust methods to engineer complex multi-particle entangled states faster than standard adiabatic protocols in Rydberg arrays.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic diagram of a bilayer neutral atom array, with one layer holding single atoms assembled at sites in a triangular optical lattice while the other in a honeycomb lattice. (b) Four fully connected atoms with an equidistant interaction strength V between arbitrary two. (c) Atomic spin transition being identically driven by a periodic amplitude-modulated field, from the ground state |0⟩to the Rydberg state |1⟩with a Floquet Rabi frequency Ω(t). (d) One-dimensional synthetic dimension in DSL with the individually addressable nearest-neighbor hopping rates.

FIG. 2. Complete Rabi-like oscillations between two adjacent DSL sites |2, n−2⟩and |2, n−1⟩with the initial state |2, n−2⟩, with n increasing from the uppermost panel n = 0 to the lowest n = 3, respectively.

FIG. 3. Full RAB processes through population transfers from |2, −2⟩to |2, 2⟩in different ways based on the PST method. (a) Four steps via four times of 1-link hops. (b) Two steps via two times of 2-link hops. (c) Two steps via one 3- link hop plus one 1-link hop. (d) One step via one 4-link hop. Detailed parameters and descriptions are found in the main text.

FIG. 4. (a) Population of |1111⟩at the instant t = T versus the duration T, based on the conventional scheme and the soft control scheme with the Gaussian envelopes in eq. (11). (b) Time-dependent populations of the five Dicke states during the transition from the ground state to the full RAB state. Effect of disorder intensity on the final population of |1111⟩ for (c) off-diagonal terms and (d) diagonal terms, with com- parison between the soft control scheme and the 4-link PST scheme. Each datum in (c) and (d) is obtained by averaging 501 results with the off-diagonal or diagonal disorder terms being randomly picked.

FIG. 5. Instantaneous energy spectrum of the five-site DSL under (a) the conventional modulation and (b) the Gaussian soft-control scheme. (c)–(e) Spatial distributions of the gap state on the five sites at t = T/4, T/2, and 3T/4 for the Gaussian scheme.
